title: Risk Management, Emergency Preparedness, and Business Continuity

content.text: Risk identification is an ongoing process involving input from multiple stakeholders. The Principal works with the Senior Leadership Team, GM/Foundation Representative, Yayasan, staff, and external advisors to scan the environment for emerging risks.

items: Curriculum gaps affecting Cambridge exam results or national curriculum compliance
Assessment malpractice or irregularities
Poor teaching quality impacting student learning outcomes
Failure to meet Cambridge or CIS/WASC standards
Low enrollment or student attrition affecting program viability
Digital divide limiting access to online learning

content.text: See Section 7: Safeguarding, Child Protection, and Wellbeing for detailed safeguarding risk management.
Once risks are identified, they must be assessed for likelihood and potential impact. This assessment determines priority and informs mitigation strategies.
Use the following 5×5 matrix to evaluate each identified risk: Likelihood Scale:

steps: Rare: May occur only in exceptional circumstances (\<10% chance in 5 years)
Unlikely: Could occur at some time (10-30% chance in 5 years)
Possible: Might occur at some time (30-60% chance in 5 years)
Likely: Will probably occur (60-85% chance in 5 years)
Almost Certain: Expected to occur (\>85% chance in 5 years)
content.title: Risk Assessment Matrix

steps: Insignificant: No significant impact on operations or reputation; minor first aid only
Minor: Short-term disruption manageable with existing resources; temporary negative attention
Moderate: Medium-term disruption requiring external support; notable negative attention; injuries requiring medical treatment
Major: Long-term disruption; significant stakeholder concern; hospitalization required; regulatory intervention
Catastrophic: Permanent disruption; potential school closure; fatality or permanent disability; criminal prosecution

content.text: Step 1: Identify the Risk Clearly describe the risk event and its potential manifestations Example: "Earthquake during school hours causing structural damage and injuries" Step 2: Assess Likelihood Consider historical data, expert advice, local conditions Example: In Jakarta, earthquakes of moderate intensity are "Possible" (rating: 3) Step 3: Assess Potential Impact Consider worst-case scenario across multiple dimensions: safety, learning, operations, reputation Example: Major earthquake could cause "Major" to "Catastrophic" impact (rating: 4-5) Step 4: Calculate Initial Risk Score Multiply likelihood × impact Example: 3 × 4 \= 12 (High risk requiring immediate mitigation) Step 5: Identify Existing Controls Document current measures that reduce likelihood or impact Example: Earthquake-resistant building design, emergency response plan, regular drills Step 6: Assess Residual Risk Re-evaluate likelihood and impact considering existing controls Example: With controls, impact might reduce to "Moderate" (3), giving residual risk of 3 × 3 \= 9 (Medium) Step 7: Determine Additional Mitigation If residual risk is unacceptable, identify further controls needed Example: Strengthen evacuation procedures, improve emergency supplies, increase drill frequency
content.title: Risk Assessment Steps

content.text: The school risk register is a living document that records all identified risks, their assessments, control measures, ownership, and monitoring schedules. The register should be maintained in EduNav and updated regularly.

items: Risk ID: Unique identifier (e.g., ACA-001, OPS-023, SAF-015)
Risk Category: From categories above
Risk Description: Clear statement of the risk event and potential consequences
Date Identified: When the risk was first added to the register
Risk Owner: Individual responsible for monitoring and managing the risk (usually a member of SLT)
Likelihood (Initial): Rating 1-5 before controls
Impact (Initial): Rating 1-5 before controls
Initial Risk Score: Likelihood × Impact
Current Controls: Existing measures to prevent or mitigate the risk
Likelihood (Residual): Rating 1-5 with current controls
Impact (Residual): Rating 1-5 with current controls
Residual Risk Score: Likelihood × Impact after controls
Risk Status: Target (acceptable), Tolerate (accept for now), Treat (mitigation planned), Transfer (insurance/contract), Terminate (eliminate activity)
Mitigation Actions: Additional measures needed to reduce residual risk
Action Owner: Person responsible for implementing mitigation
Target Completion: Deadline for mitigation actions
Monitoring Frequency: How often risk is reviewed (monthly, quarterly, annually)
Last Reviewed: Date of most recent review
Next Review: Date of next scheduled review
Notes/Updates: Running log of changes, incidents, or relevant information
content.title: Risk Register Template

content.text: Risk ID: SAF-008 Category: Health, Safety & Security Description: Major earthquake during school hours causing building damage, injuries, and inability to evacuate safely Date Identified: 15 August 2024 Risk Owner: Principal Initial Assessment (no controls):

items: Likelihood: 3 (Possible)
Impact: 5 (Catastrophic)
Initial Score: 15 (Critical)
content.title: Example Risk Register Entry

items: Building meets Indonesian earthquake-resistant standards (IMB certification current)
Emergency response plan includes earthquake protocols
Monthly earthquake drills (Drop-Cover-Hold On)
Emergency supplies and first aid kits in each building
Designated evacuation routes and assembly points
Staff training on emergency procedures (100% completion annually)
Communication system for parent notifications
Emergency contact list maintained and tested quarterly
Likelihood: 3 (Possible \- cannot prevent earthquakes)
Impact: 3 (Moderate \- controls reduce injury/casualty risk significantly)
Residual Score: 9 (Medium)

steps: Conduct annual structural safety audit (due: December 2025)
Increase drill frequency to bi-monthly for high-enrollment periods
Update emergency supplies quarterly
Provide earthquake safety training to all new students during orientation

content.text: Action Owners: Facilities Manager (structural audit), Principal (drills), Clinic Coordinator (supplies), Admissions Coordinator (new student training) Monitoring Frequency: Quarterly review; immediate review after any seismic event Last Reviewed: 15 October 2025 Next Review: 15 January 2026
Risk appetite describes the amount and type of risk the school is willing to accept in pursuit of its educational mission. Different stakeholders may have different risk appetites:

items: Yayasan: Generally risk-averse regarding financial sustainability, legal compliance, and reputation
Eduversal Central: Low tolerance for risks affecting QMS standards, exam integrity, or accreditation
GM/Foundation Representative: Balanced approach weighing educational innovation against operational stability
Principal: Must balance excellence and innovation with safety and compliance

content.text: Zero Tolerance Risks (must be eliminated or transferred):

items: Child safeguarding failures
Cambridge exam malpractice
Serious health and safety violations
Financial fraud or corruption
Legal non-compliance that could result in school closure
Discrimination or human rights violations
content.title: Defined Risk Tolerance Levels

content.text: Low Tolerance Risks (minimized to the greatest extent possible):

items: Student or staff injuries
Data protection breaches
Reputational damage
Regulatory non-compliance
Learning disruptions
Staff misconduct

content.text: Moderate Tolerance Risks (acceptable if properly managed):

items: Experimental teaching approaches (with appropriate safeguards)
New technology implementations (with pilots and support)
Curriculum innovations (within Cambridge/national frameworks)
Facility improvements (with proper planning and contractor oversight)
Extracurricular expansions (with risk assessments)

content.text: Higher Tolerance Risks (acceptable with clear benefits):

items: Competitive academic challenges (olympiads, competitions)
International partnerships and exchanges (with due diligence)
Educational research or pilot programs
Entrepreneurial student activities (with supervision)

content.text: Clear ownership is essential for effective risk management. Every risk in the register must have a named owner responsible for:

items: Monitoring the risk and its indicators
Implementing and maintaining control measures
Reporting changes in risk level
Responding when risk events occur
Recommending mitigation actions
Ultimate accountability for all school-level risks
Direct ownership of critical and high risks
Safeguarding and child protection risks
Strategic and reputational risks
Cambridge Centre compliance risks
Crisis management and emergency response

items: Financial risks and budget management

content.text: Effective communication ensures that risks are addressed at the appropriate level and that senior decision-makers have the information needed for strategic oversight.

items: Critical incidents (safeguarding, serious injury, security threats)
Immediate reporting to Principal; Principal informs GM/Foundation Representative immediately
content.title: Reporting Cadence

items: High risks with active mitigation in progress
Reported in weekly Principal-GM meetings
New risks identified during the week
All high and critical risks reviewed
Updates to risk register in EduNav
Summary report to Yayasan if critical risks exist
Comprehensive review of entire risk register
Medium risks assessed and updated
Trend analysis (are risks increasing/decreasing?)
Report to Yayasan Board
Complete risk register review and validation
Environmental scan for emerging risks
Integration with strategic planning and budget cycles
Comprehensive report to Yayasan and Eduversal Central

content.text: Escalate risks to higher levels when:

steps: Risk Score Increases: A risk moves from one category to a higher category (e.g., Medium to High)
Control Failure: Existing controls prove ineffective or fail
Near-Miss Event: An incident that could have been serious occurs
External Change: Regulatory changes, natural disasters, or other external factors increase risk
Resource Constraints: Additional budget or expertise needed beyond school authority
Cross-School Impact: Risk could affect other Eduversal partner schools
Stakeholder Concern: Parents, staff, or community raise significant concerns
Media Attention: Risk becomes subject of public or press scrutiny
content.title: Escalation Triggers

items: Medium → High Risk: Principal notifies GM/Foundation Representative; SLT meeting scheduled within 48 hours
High → Critical Risk: Principal notifies GM/Foundation Representative and Yayasan leadership immediately; emergency meeting within 24 hours; Eduversal Central informed
Critical Risk Event: Principal activates crisis management protocol (see Section 15.3)

Comparison reports across time periods or partner schools
Principal: Full access to all school risks
GM/Foundation Representative: View all risks, edit financial/HR risks
SLT members: Edit risks in their portfolios
Risk owners: View and update assigned risks
Yayasan Board: View-only access to high and critical risks

subsections.title: Risk Management Cycle and Continuous Improvement

content.text: Risk management is not a one-time exercise but a continuous cycle: 1. Identify → 2. Assess → 3. Control → 4. Monitor → 5. Review → 1. Identify (repeat)

items: Every incident, accident, or near-miss triggers a review of related risks
Root cause analysis identifies whether risk controls were adequate
Register updated with lessons learned
content.title: Continuous Improvement Mechanisms

items: Post-drill debriefs capture improvement opportunities
Exercise scenarios test effectiveness of risk controls
Findings integrated into mitigation action plans
Comparison with other Eduversal partner schools
Adoption of best practices from CIS/WASC network
Learning from incidents at similar schools globally
Regular safety surveys and wellbeing checks
Suggestion boxes and anonymous reporting channels
Student council and staff representative input
Periodic audits by health and safety consultants
Insurance company risk assessments
Legal and regulatory compliance reviews
Cambridge Centre inspections and quality reviews

content.text: While risk management aims to prevent crises, emergency response planning prepares the school to act effectively when incidents occur despite preventive measures. This section details the structures, procedures, and protocols for responding to emergencies that threaten safety, disrupt operations, or require immediate intervention.
Not all incidents require the same level of response. The school uses a four-level classification system to ensure proportionate and effective action.
Definition: Localized event with minimal impact, manageable within normal operations Examples:

items: Minor student injury (scraped knee, small cut)
Small classroom spill of non-hazardous materials
Brief power outage with backup systems functioning
Minor student behavioral incident
content.title: Level 1: Minor Incident

items: Handled by classroom teacher or immediate supervisor
Standard first aid or maintenance response
Incident logged in EduNav
Parent notification if student injury
No need to activate emergency response team

content.text: Definition: Event requiring coordinated response but not threatening life or major operations Examples:

items: Student injury requiring medical attention beyond first aid
Localized fire contained by fire extinguisher
Hazardous materials spill requiring cleanup protocol
Serious student behavioral incident (fight, property damage)
Extended utility failure affecting multiple areas
Security concern (suspicious person on campus)
content.title: Level 2: Significant Incident

items: Notify Principal or Deputy Principal immediately
Activate relevant response protocols (fire, medical, security)
Contain incident and secure area
Document thoroughly in EduNav
Parent communication as appropriate
Incident review within 24 hours
Update risk register if pattern emerges

content.text: Definition: Serious event potentially affecting multiple people or operations, requiring full emergency response Examples:

items: Earthquake, fire, or flood affecting significant portions of campus
Serious injury or medical emergency (heart attack, severe allergic reaction, major trauma)
Intruder on campus or security lockdown
Gas leak or other hazardous materials emergency
Missing student (especially from boarding facility)
Major transportation accident involving school vehicles
Violent incident or threat
content.title: Level 3: Major Emergency

items: Immediate: Principal (or most senior leader on site) assumes Incident Commander role
Activate Emergency Response Team
Contact emergency services (police, fire, ambulance) as needed
Implement relevant emergency protocol (evacuation, lockdown, shelter-in-place)
Account for all students and staff
Notify GM/Foundation Representative
Activate crisis communications plan
Parent notifications as soon as safely possible
Secure scene and preserve evidence if needed
Document everything
Post-incident debrief and report to Yayasan within 48 hours

content.text: Definition: Catastrophic event threatening life, requiring external emergency services, potential for media attention Examples:

items: Fatality or multiple serious injuries
Major natural disaster (severe earthquake, tsunami warning)
Fire requiring evacuation of entire campus
Terrorist attack or armed threat
Serious crime (assault, sexual offense)
Major structural collapse
Epidemic outbreak requiring school closure
Multiple missing students
Any incident likely to attract media attention
content.title: Level 4: Critical Crisis

items: Immediate: Principal activates full Crisis Management Protocol
Emergency services called immediately (112 for Indonesia)
Emergency Response Team and Crisis Communication Team fully mobilized
Immediate notification to GM/Foundation Representative, Yayasan leadership, and Eduversal Central
Implementation of emergency action plan (evacuation, lockdown, or shelter-in-place)
Establish command center and communication hub
Account for all students and staff; ongoing safety monitoring
Activate parent communication tree
Coordinate with emergency services and authorities
Media liaison designated (usually GM/Foundation Representative or designated spokesperson)
Psychological first aid and counseling mobilized
Business continuity plan activated if operations disrupted
Daily situation reports to Yayasan and Eduversal Central
Comprehensive post-crisis review and formal investigation

content.text: The Emergency Response Team (ERT) is a pre-designated group of staff members trained to coordinate the school's response to emergencies. The team structure provides clear roles and ensures effective action even if some members are unavailable.
Incident Commander (Primary: Principal | Backup: Deputy Principal/Senior SLT member) Responsibilities:

items: Assume overall command and control of emergency response
Assess situation and declare emergency level
Activate appropriate emergency protocols
Authorize evacuation, lockdown, or other protective actions
Coordinate with external emergency services
Make critical decisions about student/staff safety
Authorize communications to parents and external parties
Ensure accountability of all students and staff
Declare "all clear" when emergency has passed
content.title: Core ERT Roles

content.text: Communication Officer (Primary: Designated SLT member | Backup: Admissions/Marketing staff) Responsibilities:

items: Activate communication tree to notify staff
Coordinate parent notifications via SMS, email, phone calls
Draft communication messages with Incident Commander approval
Manage incoming calls and queries
Coordinate with media liaison if external communications needed
Update school website and social media as appropriate
Document all communications sent and received
Maintain communication log

content.text: First Aid Officer (Primary: School Nurse/Clinic Coordinator | Backup: Trained staff member) Responsibilities:

items: Assess and provide emergency medical care
Triage casualties if multiple injuries
Coordinate with ambulance/hospital services
Manage medical supply distribution
Track students requiring medical attention
Coordinate follow-up medical care
Communicate medical status updates to Incident Commander
Document all medical interventions

content.text: Evacuation Coordinator (Primary: Facilities Manager/Operations Manager | Backup: PE/Activities Coordinator) Responsibilities:

items: Oversee orderly evacuation if needed
Ensure evacuation routes are clear and safe
Coordinate with floor/building wardens
Account for all students and staff at assembly points
Manage student groupings by class/year
Conduct roll call and report status to Incident Commander
Coordinate shelter or alternative accommodation if needed
Manage return to buildings after "all clear"

content.text: Security Coordinator (Primary: Security Supervisor | Backup: Facilities Manager) Responsibilities:

items: Secure campus perimeter and control access
Coordinate with external security or police if needed
Manage visitor management during emergency
Preserve evidence if incident involves crime or investigation
Monitor CCTV and security systems
Control traffic flow during evacuation or emergency vehicle access
Support lockdown procedures if implemented

content.text: Student Support Coordinator (Primary: Counselor/Wellbeing Lead | Backup: Pastoral Care staff) Responsibilities:

items: Provide immediate psychological first aid to students
Identify students with special needs requiring support
Coordinate care for distressed students
Support students with special considerations (anxiety, trauma history, disabilities)
Coordinate parent reunifications if needed
Plan follow-up counseling and support
Communicate with special needs families
Mobilize external mental health resources if needed

content.text: Logistics Coordinator (Primary: Operations/Finance Manager | Backup: Administrative staff) Responsibilities:

items: Manage emergency supplies and resources
Coordinate food, water, shelter needs if prolonged emergency
Arrange transportation if evacuation from campus required
Procure additional resources as needed
Coordinate with vendors/contractors
Document expenses related to emergency
Support accommodation needs (e.g., if students cannot return to boarding houses)
Manage insurance claims if applicable

content.text: Documentation Officer (Primary: Administrative staff | Backup: Designated SLT member) Responsibilities:

items: Document timeline of events
Photograph/video evidence of damage or conditions (if safe)
Collect witness statements
Maintain incident log
Coordinate with authorities for official reports
Preserve documentation for investigation or insurance
Support post-incident review
Update records in EduNav

content.text: Automatic Activation: Certain events trigger immediate ERT activation:

items: Fire alarm activation
Earthquake or natural disaster
Intruder alert or security threat
Any Level 3 or Level 4 emergency
content.title: ERT Activation Protocol

items: Any staff member can request ERT activation by notifying the Principal or calling the emergency hotline
Principal or most senior leader on site assesses and decides whether to activate full ERT or partial response
Emergency SMS to all ERT members with code (e.g., "CODE RED \- ERT Assemble")
Public address announcement on campus
Emergency phone tree if technology fails
Primary: Principal's office or designated command center
Secondary: Pre-designated alternate location if primary is inaccessible
ERT members report immediately upon notification
ERT operates continuously until Incident Commander declares "stand down"
Shift rotations for prolonged emergencies
Backup members assume roles if primary members unavailable or fatigued

subsections.title: Emergency Types and Specific Response Protocols

content.text: Each type of emergency requires specific actions tailored to its unique characteristics. The following protocols provide detailed guidance for the most common emergency scenarios in Indonesian school contexts.

steps: Discovery: Any person discovering fire immediately:
content.title: Fire Emergency

items: Activates nearest fire alarm pull station
Calls emergency services (113 for fire in Indonesia)
Only attempts to extinguish if fire is small and safe to do so using PASS technique (Pull pin, Aim at base, Squeeze handle, Sweep side to side)

steps: Alarm Activation: When fire alarm sounds:

items: All staff: Stop all activities immediately
Teachers: Direct students to line up at classroom door in silence
Specialized areas: Lab, workshop, clinic, kitchen staff secure hazardous materials if time permits (30 seconds maximum)

steps: Evacuation:

items: Follow designated evacuation routes (posted in each room)
Use secondary routes if primary route is blocked by smoke or fire
Close doors and windows if time permits (slows fire spread)
Feel doors before opening; use alternative route if door is hot
Stay low if smoke present (clean air near floor)
Do not use elevators under any circumstances
Assist students with disabilities (buddy system pre-established)

steps: Assembly:

items: Proceed to designated assembly points (located away from buildings)
Teachers conduct roll call using class lists
Report missing persons immediately to Evacuation Coordinator
Remain at assembly point until "all clear" from Incident Commander

steps: Emergency Services Coordination:

items: Incident Commander meets fire brigade at gate
Provide building plans and information about missing persons
Identify hazards (gas lines, chemical storage)
Keep students and non-essential personnel away from operational area

steps: All Clear and Re-entry:

items: Only Incident Commander can authorize re-entry
Inspect buildings before allowing occupancy
Check for structural damage, smoke damage, water damage
Document damage thoroughly
Boarding Schools: Night-time fire alarm triggers residential staff response; students evacuate in sleepwear if necessary; blankets and emergency supplies at assembly points
Science Labs: Never return to lab to retrieve experiments or materials
Special Needs: Evacuation assistants pre-assigned for students with mobility issues; visual/auditory alarms for deaf/blind students
Islamic Boarding Schools: Female students may need to bring hijab/modest covering; assembly point considerations for gender separation if culturally appropriate

steps: Immediate Response: When shaking begins:
content.title: Earthquake Emergency

items: Drop: Get down on hands and knees immediately (prevents being knocked over)
Cover: Get under a sturdy desk, table, or against an interior wall; cover head and neck with arms
Hold On: Hold onto furniture/cover until shaking stops; be prepared for furniture to move

steps: Specific Location Guidelines:

items: Classroom: Under desk, away from windows
Hallway: Against interior wall in duck-cover position
Playground/Outdoor: Move away from buildings, trees, utility poles; drop to ground
Cafeteria: Under tables if possible; away from kitchen with falling hazards
Science Lab: Away from glass, chemicals, equipment; under benches if possible
Mosque/Prayer Hall: Away from chandeliers, structural pillars; cover head
Vehicles: Stop safely; stay inside vehicle; avoid overpasses, power lines, trees

steps: What NOT to Do:

items: Do not run outside during shaking (falling debris danger)
Do not use doorways (modern building myth; doorways are not stronger)
Do not use elevators
Do not go near windows or glass

steps: Assessment:

items: Incident Commander evaluates safety of buildings
Look for obvious structural damage, gas leaks, fire
Decision: Evacuate or Shelter-in-Place

steps: If Evacuation Ordered:

items: Follow evacuation procedures for fire
Watch for aftershocks; be ready to drop-cover-hold on again
Avoid damaged areas of campus
Assembly points should be in open areas away from buildings and overhead hazards

steps: If Shelter-in-Place Ordered:

items: Remain in buildings if no apparent structural damage
Move away from windows and external walls
Conduct roll call in place
Prepare for possible evacuation if aftershock causes damage

steps: Hazard Assessment:

items: Facilities staff check for:
Structural cracks or damage
Gas leaks (smell, listen for hissing)
Electrical hazards (sparks, exposed wires)
Water line breaks
Chemical spills
Fire hazards
Mark unsafe areas clearly; restrict access

steps: Communication:

items: Parent notification: School safe, students accounted for, further updates to follow
Update every 30-60 minutes until situation stable
Coordinate pickup procedures if early dismissal needed

steps: Aftershocks:

items: Expect aftershocks; may be hours or days later
Each aftershock: Drop-Cover-Hold On
Monitor Indonesian geophysical agency (BMKG) for updates
Night-time earthquakes: Students shelter in place in dorms initially
Residential staff conduct roll calls room by room
Evacuation to outdoor assembly only if structural concerns
Counseling support for traumatized students
Accommodation arrangements if buildings unsafe

content.text: Flooding can result from heavy rainfall (common in Jakarta and other Indonesian cities), drainage failures, or nearby river overflow. Pre-Flood Preparation (Monsoon Season Readiness):

steps: Monitor weather forecasts and flood warnings from BMKG
Inspect drainage systems; clear debris
Position sandbags at vulnerable entry points
Elevate critical equipment (servers, electrical panels)
Prepare emergency supplies on upper floors
Review evacuation routes to higher ground
content.title: Flooding Emergency

steps: Early Warning: If flash flood warning or heavy rain:

items: Move students to upper floors preemptively
Shut down electrical systems in lower levels
Secure valuable equipment and documents
Activate flood response plan

steps: Rising Water:

items: If water enters buildings: Evacuate to upper floors immediately
Do not attempt to wade through moving water (15cm can knock adult off feet)
Avoid electrocution: Turn off power; assume standing water is electrified
Close doors/windows to slow water entry
Account for all students and staff on safe floors

steps: Trapped by Flooding:

items: If evacuation impossible: Shelter on highest floors
Call emergency services (112) and report location, number of people, conditions
Prepare rooftop access for helicopter rescue if necessary
Ration emergency supplies; conserve phone batteries

steps: Evacuation Through Flood Water:

items: Only if life-threatening emergency (fire, structural collapse)
Link arms or hold ropes to stay together
Test depth with stick; avoid fast-moving water
Watch for hidden hazards (open manholes, debris, animals)

steps: Do Not Return Until Authorized: Incident Commander assesses safety
Hazards:

items: Structural damage from water pressure
Contaminated water (sewage, chemicals, disease)
Electrical hazards
Mold and health hazards
Slippery surfaces

steps: Cleanup:

items: Professional cleaning company for sewage-contaminated areas
Document damage for insurance
Dispose of contaminated food, materials
Dry and disinfect thoroughly

steps: Health Monitoring: Watch for waterborne disease; encourage handwashing, avoid contact with floodwater

content.text: Priority: Preserve Life → Prevent Deterioration → Promote Recovery Immediate Response:

steps: Assess Scene Safety: Ensure area safe for responders
Primary Survey (ABCs):
content.title: Medical Emergency (Serious Injury or Illness)

items: Airway: Is airway open?
Breathing: Is person breathing adequately?
Circulation: Is there pulse/severe bleeding?
Disability: Level of consciousness? Spinal injury suspected?

steps: Call for Help:

items: Emergency services (118 or 119 for ambulance in Indonesia)
School clinic/First Aid Officer
Notify Principal immediately
Cardiac Arrest: Begin CPR immediately; deploy AED (automated external defibrillator)
Severe Bleeding: Apply direct pressure; elevate wound; pressure points if needed; tourniquet only as last resort
Choking: Abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver) for conscious victim; CPR if unconscious
Severe Allergic Reaction (Anaphylaxis):
Administer EpiPen if available and prescribed to student
Call ambulance immediately
Position: lying down with legs elevated unless breathing difficulty (then sitting)
Monitor and be ready to perform CPR
Second dose of epinephrine may be given after 5-15 minutes if no improvement
Poisoning: Call poison control; do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed
Severe Burns: Cool burn with running water 20 minutes; cover with clean cloth; treat for shock
Spinal Injury Suspected: Do not move victim unless in immediate danger; stabilize head and neck
Fractures: Immobilize; ice; elevate if possible; medical evaluation needed
Head Injury with Loss of Consciousness: Do not move; monitor breathing and consciousness; medical evaluation required
Seizures:
Protect from injury (move dangerous objects away)
Cushion head; turn on side if possible
Time seizure; if longer than 5 minutes or multiple seizures, call ambulance
Do not restrain or put anything in mouth
Stay with student until fully conscious; position on side during recovery
Asthma Attack:
Help student use inhaler (puff \+ spacer if available)
Sit upright; stay calm
If no improvement after 10-15 minutes or worsening, call ambulance
Diabetic Emergency:
Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar): Give fast-acting sugar (juice, candy) if conscious
Hyperglycemia: Requires medical treatment; call ambulance if severe symptoms
Time of incident and discovery
Student name, age, class
Symptoms and assessment findings
Actions taken (first aid, medications given, etc.)
Time ambulance called and arrived
Hospital transported to
Staff members involved
Parent notification time and response
Immediate call to parents for any emergency requiring ambulance transport
Provide factual information: what happened, injuries/symptoms, actions taken, current status
Hospital information and how parents can proceed
Staff member accompanies student to hospital if parent cannot arrive immediately
Incident report completed within 24 hours
Parent meeting to discuss incident and any ongoing care needs
Return-to-school medical clearance if hospitalization occurred
Care plan for students with ongoing medical needs
Staff debrief if traumatic incident
Low Threat: Unauthorized person on campus but no indication of harmful intent
Medium Threat: Suspicious behavior, verbal threats, person appears agitated or unstable
High Threat: Weapon visible, active violence, credible threat of harm
content.title: Security Threat or Intruder

steps: Security or staff approach person calmly
Ask purpose of visit; escort to visitor check-in
If cooperative: Complete visitor procedures
If uncooperative: Ask to leave; call security backup
Notify Principal if person refuses to leave
Call police if trespassing continues
Do not approach; maintain distance
Call security and Principal immediately
Describe person, location, behavior
Activate soft lockdown if appropriate:

items: Classes continue but doors locked
No outdoor activities; students remain in buildings
No campus movement unless essential
Heightened awareness

steps: Security assesses situation; calls police if needed
Notify GM/Foundation Representative
Hold soft lockdown until threat resolved
Parent notification if extended
Immediate Alert: "LOCKDOWN LOCKDOWN LOCKDOWN" over PA system or emergency notification
All Staff and Students:

items: Move inside nearest secure room immediately
Lock doors; barricade with furniture if possible
Close windows and blinds
Turn off lights
Silence phones (silent mode, not vibrate)
Move away from doors and windows; hide if possible
Sit on floor in silence
Do not open door for anyone until "all clear" from verified authority

steps: If Outside During Lockdown:

items: Move away from buildings toward exit or safe area
Run in zig-zag pattern if necessary
Do not gather in large groups
Leave campus if safe to do so; seek secure location

steps: If Unable to Secure Room:

items: Evacuate building if safe exit available
Hide in inaccessible location (locked closet, etc.)
As absolute last resort: Prepare to defend or flee if confronted
Incident Commander coordinates with police
Remain in lockdown until police give "all clear"
Cooperate fully with police; provide information
Police will clear building room by room; follow instructions exactly
Comprehensive security review
Counseling for affected students and staff
Family meetings
Communication plan for community
Potential media management
Long-term security improvements
Residential buildings included in lockdown
Security patrol of dormitories
24/7 security presence
Communication with parents may be delayed if incident occurs at night; update as soon as safe to do so

steps: Immediate:
content.title: Hazardous Materials Incident

items: Evacuate lab immediately if vapors, large spill, or unknown substance
Close door; post warning sign
Alert lab technician and Principal
Activate chemical spill response team if available

steps: Small Spill (Minor, Known, Safe):

items: Trained staff use spill kit to contain and neutralize
Ventilate area
Dispose of contaminated materials per regulations
Document incident

steps: Large Spill or Dangerous Chemical:

items: Evacuate building or area
Call fire brigade (hazmat team)
Provide SDS (Safety Data Sheet) to responders
Account for all persons
Medical evaluation for anyone exposed

steps: If Gas Smell Detected:

items: Evacuate building immediately (do not activate fire alarm; electrical spark risk)
No smoking, flames, or sparks
Do not use electrical switches, phones, or electronics
Call gas company emergency line and fire brigade from safe location
Open windows if safe to do so
Shut off gas supply at meter if accessible and safe

steps: Do Not Re-Enter: Until gas company declares safe

content.text: External Hazmat Incident (e.g., industrial accident nearby):

steps: Shelter-in-Place if airborne contaminants:

items: Close all windows and doors
Turn off HVAC to prevent outside air intake
Seal gaps with wet towels if possible
Move to interior rooms away from windows
Monitor radio/news for updates from authorities
Do not evacuate unless instructed by authorities (exposure risk)

steps: Evacuation if authorities order:

items: Follow designated evacuation routes away from hazard
Transport arrangements for students if needed

content.text: Effective evacuation depends on clear procedures practiced regularly through drills.

items: Fire alarm activation (automatic evacuation)
Earthquake causes structural concerns
Gas leak or hazmat incident
Bomb threat (after assessment)
Flood water entering buildings
Any situation where building occupancy is unsafe
content.title: Evacuation Decision-Making

items: External hazmat incident with airborne contaminants
Severe weather (tornado, extreme lightning)
External security threat (safer inside)
Earthquake with no structural damage (await aftershock assessment)

steps: Immediately direct students to line up at door
Take class register/roll book
Conduct quick visual sweep of room (check restrooms, under desks)
Close windows and doors (but do not lock)
Turn off lights if time permits
Lead class via designated route to assembly point
Keep students together as cohesive group
Conduct roll call at assembly point
Report status (all present / missing students) to Evacuation Coordinator
Supervise students until "all clear" or further instructions
content.title: Evacuation Roles and Responsibilities

content.text: Floor/Building Wardens (designated staff for each building/floor):

steps: Hear alarm; begin floor/area sweep
Check all rooms (classrooms, restrooms, offices, closets)
Direct any remaining occupants to evacuate
Check stairwells are clear
Close fire doors if applicable
Be last to leave floor/building
Report to Incident Commander: "Floor/Building [X] clear" or report issues
Shut down gas lines if safe to do so
Ensure fire exits and routes are accessible
Assist with evacuating persons with disabilities if needed
Bring building plans to Incident Commander
Stand by to support emergency services
Control campus access; prevent entry during evacuation
Direct traffic flow away from buildings
Keep evacuation routes clear
Assist with crowd control at assembly points
Coordinate with external emergency responders
Bring emergency communication devices (phones, megaphone)
Bring emergency contact lists
Support Communication Officer with parent notifications
Bring emergency supplies to assembly point if time permits

content.text: Assembly points are pre-designated open areas at safe distance from buildings: Primary Assembly Points:

items: Early Years/Primary: Front field area away from buildings
Secondary: Main sports field or designated open area
Boarding Students: Separate assembly area near residential buildings
Staff Only: Designated section of assembly area
content.title: Assembly Points

items: Minimum 50 meters from buildings
Away from overhead hazards (trees, power lines)
Avoid areas near gas lines or underground utilities
Accessible from multiple evacuation routes
Clear sight lines for supervision
Space for entire school population with class groupings
Accessible surface (not muddy or uneven)
Shaded if possible (tropical heat considerations)

content.text: Secondary Assembly Points (use if primary blocked or unsafe):

items: Alternative open areas on campus
Off-campus locations (e.g., nearby park, mosque, community center)
Pre-arranged through MOUs with neighboring properties
Class flags or signs for easy student grouping
First aid supplies
Emergency contact lists
Communication devices
Water and basic supplies if extended evacuation
Shade structures if available
Hold hands with buddy; teacher leads, assistant at rear
Use simple evacuation song or chant to keep calm and together
Extra attention to ensuring all students accounted for
Comfort items (favorite toy, blanket) acceptable if time permits
Parents may need to pick up immediately; controlled reunification process
content.title: Evacuation Procedures by School Section

items: Line up quickly and quietly; walk briskly but do not run
Buddy system (accountability partners)
Older students may assist younger
Teacher briefs students on reason for evacuation if age-appropriate
Students expected to be more independent; move quickly and responsibly
May need to evacuate from subject classrooms with non-homeroom teachers
Roll call by subject class teacher at assembly point; cross-referenced with homeroom lists
Older students may be assigned specific roles (assisting younger students, carrying supplies)
Nighttime evacuations: Emergency lighting; clear signage
Students evacuate in nightwear if necessary; blankets at assembly point
Residential staff conduct roll call by dormitory
Male/female assembly areas separate if culturally appropriate
Individualized evacuation plans for students with mobility, sensory, or cognitive challenges
Assigned evacuation assistants (buddy \+ staff)
Specialized equipment if needed (evacuation chair for multi-story buildings)
Visual and auditory alarms for deaf/hard of hearing students
Simple, direct language for students with cognitive disabilities
Extra support for students with anxiety disorders (familiar adult, comfort objects)

steps: Class Teachers: Conduct roll call immediately upon arrival at assembly point using class register
Report Status: Inform Evacuation Coordinator:
content.title: Accounting for All Students and Staff

items: "Class [X] all present"
"Class [Y] missing [number] students: [names]"

steps: Specialist Teachers: Report status of students in their classes at time of alarm
Administrative Check: Cross-reference reports to ensure no duplicates or gaps
Staff Roll Call: Administrative staff account for all adult staff members
Evacuation Coordinator immediately informs Incident Commander of missing persons
Verification: Confirm missing person was on campus (check attendance records, sign-in logs)
If Confirmed On Campus and Missing:

items: Notify emergency services immediately
Provide name, age, last known location, description, photo if available
Floor/Building Wardens conduct secondary search if safe to do so
Parents of missing student(s) notified immediately

steps: If Person Located: Report immediately to Incident Commander and Evacuation Coordinator; update emergency services

content.text: "All Clear" Declaration: Only Incident Commander can authorize return Inspection Before Re-entry:

items: Facilities team inspects for structural damage, hazards, contamination
Emergency services give clearance if they responded
Utilities functional and safe (power, gas, water)
Environment safe (air quality, temperature, no smoke/fumes)
content.title: Controlled Return to Buildings

steps: Small inspection team enters first
If safe, staff enter to prepare classrooms
Students return by section or class group (avoids congestion)
Resume normal operations or adjust schedule as needed

items: Activate business continuity plan (see Section 15.3)
Arrange alternative location or early dismissal
Parent communication regarding pickup or alternate arrangements

content.text: Effective communication during emergencies requires clear roles, pre-planned messages, and prioritized stakeholder groups.

steps: Speed: Communicate as soon as safely possible; even "we are aware and assessing" is valuable
Accuracy: Only share confirmed facts; avoid speculation
Consistency: All spokespersons use same information; no contradictions
Transparency: Share what you know and what you don't know; don't hide bad news
Empathy: Acknowledge fear, concern, and impact on community
Action-Oriented: Tell stakeholders what you are doing and what they should do
Continuity: Update regularly even if no new information; silence creates rumors
content.title: Communication Principles During Crisis

content.text: Incident Commander: Authorizes all communications; approves messages Communication Officer: Drafts and sends communications GM/Foundation Representative: Primary spokesperson for media and external parties Principal: May serve as spokesperson for internal community (parents, staff) depending on situation Eduversal Central Communications: Supports with template messages, media relations, cross-partner school coordination
content.title: Crisis Communication Team

steps: Emergency Services: If called, provide situation report
On-Site Staff: Briefing on situation and required actions
GM/Foundation Representative: Immediate notification of Level 3 or 4 emergencies
Yayasan Leadership: For Level 3 or 4 emergencies
content.title: Stakeholder Communication Priority

content.text: Urgent (within 1-2 hours): 5. Parents of Directly Affected Students: Individual calls for students injured, hospitalized, or directly involved 6. All Parents: General notification of incident, status of students, school actions 7. Eduversal Central: Formal incident report 8. All Staff Not On Site: Information about incident and expectations Important (within 3-6 hours): 9. Yayasan Board: Formal briefing 10. Relevant Authorities: Indonesian education ministry, Cambridge (if exam-related), health authorities (if health incident) 11. Media: If inquiries or if public interest; coordinated response only Follow-Up (within 24 hours): 12. School Community: Detailed update on situation, next steps, support available 13. Partner Schools: Eduversal network informed if lessons learned or risks relevant to them

items: Primary: SMS blast (fastest; highest open rate in Indonesia)
Secondary: Email with more details
Tertiary: WhatsApp groups (if established)
Phone Calls: For parents of directly affected students
School Website: Updated status page
Social Media: Brief status updates on school Facebook/Instagram
content.title: Communication Channels

items: Primary: SMS or WhatsApp to all staff
Secondary: Email for detailed information
Staff Meeting: Face-to-face briefing as soon as possible
Directly: Teachers brief students age-appropriately in classrooms once safe
Via Parents: For younger students or if school closure
Media: Designated spokesperson only; prepared statements; refer all inquiries to GM/Foundation Representative
Authorities: Principal or GM as required by regulations

content.text: Initial Parent Notification \- Incident Occurred English: "Dear Parents, we are writing to inform you of an incident at [School Name] today involving [brief description]. All students and staff are safe and accounted for. We have [actions taken]. We will provide an update within [timeframe]. Please do not come to school at this time to allow emergency personnel to work. We will notify you if early pickup is required. Thank you for your understanding." Bahasa Indonesia: "Kepada Orang Tua/Wali yang terhormat, kami menginformasikan telah terjadi insiden di [Nama Sekolah] hari ini terkait [deskripsi singkat]. Seluruh siswa dan staf dalam kondisi aman dan telah dihitung keberadaannya. Kami telah [tindakan yang diambil]. Kami akan memberikan informasi lebih lanjut dalam [jangka waktu]. Mohon untuk tidak datang ke sekolah saat ini agar petugas darurat dapat bekerja. Kami akan memberitahu jika diperlukan penjemputan lebih awal. Terima kasih atas pengertiannya." Update \- Situation Resolved English: "Update: The situation at [School Name] has been resolved. [Brief explanation of resolution]. Students have returned to class and school operations are continuing normally. No injuries occurred. We appreciate your patience. A full report will be shared [timeframe]." Bahasa Indonesia: "Informasi terbaru: Situasi di [Nama Sekolah] telah teratasi. [Penjelasan singkat]. Siswa telah kembali ke kelas dan kegiatan sekolah berjalan normal. Tidak ada yang terluka. Kami menghargai kesabaran Anda. Laporan lengkap akan dibagikan [jangka waktu]." Early Dismissal / School Closure Notification English: "Dear Parents, due to [reason], we are implementing early dismissal today at [time]. Please arrange to pick up your child at this time. Buses will run early routes. Students will be supervised until pickup. Tomorrow's school schedule [will resume normally / is cancelled / will be decided and communicated by 6 PM]. Thank you for your flexibility." Bahasa Indonesia: "Kepada Orang Tua/Wali, dikarenakan [alasan], kami melakukan penutupan sekolah lebih awal hari ini pada pukul [waktu]. Mohon untuk menjemput anak Anda pada waktu tersebut. Bus akan beroperasi lebih awal. Siswa akan diawasi hingga dijemput. Jadwal sekolah besok [akan berjalan normal / dibatalkan / akan diputuskan dan dikomunikasikan pukul 18.00]. Terima kasih atas pengertiannya."
content.title: Template Communications

content.text: Maintaining accurate, accessible emergency contact information is foundational to crisis response.

items: Parent/guardian full names
Mobile phone numbers (primary and secondary)
Emergency alternate contacts (2 minimum; not parents)
Home address
Work addresses and phone numbers
Email addresses
Preferred contact method and language
Medical information (allergies, conditions, medications)
Special instructions (custody arrangements, restraining orders)
content.title: Emergency Contact Database

content.text: Information Required for Each Staff Member:

items: Emergency contact person (relationship, phone number)
Secondary emergency contact
Medical information
Home address
Updated annually at enrollment/re-enrollment
Verified termly (each term parents confirm or update)
Stored in EduNav with appropriate access controls
Backup hard copies in Principal's office and emergency response kit
Tested annually with drill-related parent notification

content.text: As Principal, you are the ultimate point of contact for emergencies occurring at any time, including nights, weekends, holidays, and school breaks. Communication Pathways:

items: Mobile phone (personal) provided to:
GM/Foundation Representative
Deputy Principal
Security Supervisor (for overnight/weekend emergencies)
Yayasan leadership
Eduversal Central emergency contacts
Backup: If Principal unavailable, Deputy Principal assumes role with full authority
Emergency Hotline: Consider dedicated emergency phone number for serious incidents
content.title: 24/7 Principal Availability

items: Answer calls/texts within 15 minutes at all times
If attending to personal matter: Acknowledge receipt and delegate immediate response to Deputy/SLT member
For Level 3 or 4 emergencies: Drop everything and respond immediately
For Level 1 or 2: Provide guidance; may not need to come to campus
Overnight residential staff have direct line to Principal
Principal residence on or very near campus strongly preferred
Clear protocols for residential staff to handle nighttime incidents independently if immediate risk before Principal arrives
GM/Foundation Representative (by Incident Commander directly)
Senior Leadership Team members
Emergency Response Team members
content.title: Emergency Contact Tree

items: Yayasan leadership (by GM/Foundation Representative)
Eduversal Central emergency contact (by GM/Foundation Representative)
All teaching staff (by Communication Officer)
Support staff heads
Parents of all students (by Communication Officer using mass notification system)
Staff not in Tier 2
Relevant authorities (by Principal or GM as required)
Media/public (by GM/Foundation Representative if necessary)

content.text: Lockdown and shelter-in-place are protective actions that keep students and staff inside buildings for safety.
Purpose: Secure buildings and rooms to prevent access by intruder or dangerous person When Used:

items: Intruder on campus with weapon or violent behavior
Threat of violence from external source
Hostage situation
Active shooter (extremely rare but prepared)
Serious crime in progress on or near campus
content.title: Lockdown (Security Threat)

content.text: Types of Lockdown: Full/Hard Lockdown (Imminent Threat):

items: ALL doors locked immediately
ALL windows closed and covered
Lights off
Hide out of sight from doors/windows
Complete silence (phones on silent, no talking)
Do not open door for anyone until "all clear" from verified authority
Duration: Until police clear building or Incident Commander gives "all clear"
Doors locked but classes continue
Students remain in classrooms
No one enters or exits buildings
No outdoor activities
No campus movement except essential staff with radio/communication
Duration: Until threat assessed and resolved
PA System: "LOCKDOWN, LOCKDOWN, LOCKDOWN"
Alarm: Distinct sound pattern different from fire alarm
Radio/Phone: Security or Principal notifies staff

content.text: Specific Lockdown Actions by Location: In Classroom:

steps: Lock door immediately (if no lock, barricade with furniture)
Close blinds/curtains
Move students away from doors and windows to least visible area
Turn off lights; silence phones
Conduct silent roll call (headcount)
Keep students calm and quiet
Do not respond to knocks or calls unless verified "all clear"
Move to nearest classroom or secure room immediately
If no room accessible, hide in lockable bathroom stall, closet, or other space
Lock/barricade if possible
Silence phone; do not make noise
If exits accessible, evacuate from campus immediately (run in zig-zag; spread out)
If no safe exit, move to most secure portion of space (storage room, kitchen)
Barricade doors with tables, equipment
Turn off lights; hide behind cover
Silence phones
Evaluate: Can you safely evacuate campus? (Yes → RUN; No → HIDE)
If evacuating: Run away from threat; zig-zag pattern; seek safe location off campus
If cannot evacuate: Hide in location off main pathways; stay low; silence phone

items: Announced by Incident Commander only
May include specific phrase or code word to ensure legitimacy
Verified authority: Police or Principal in person at door
Counseling available for students/staff experiencing distress
Parent communication explaining incident and school response
Debrief meeting with staff
Security review and improvements

content.text: Purpose: Keep students/staff inside to avoid exposure to external environmental hazard When Used:

items: Hazmat incident nearby (chemical spill, gas release, fire with toxic smoke)
Severe weather (tornado, extreme lightning, hailstorm)
Air quality emergency (volcanic ash, extreme pollution, smoke from wildfire)
External event making evacuation more dangerous than staying inside
content.title: Shelter-in-Place (Environmental Hazard)

steps: Bring Everyone Inside:

items: Students and staff outdoors move inside immediately
Close and lock exterior doors

steps: Seal Building:

items: Close all windows and exterior doors
Turn off HVAC systems to prevent outside air intake
Seal gaps around doors/windows with wet towels or tape if serious airborne threat

steps: Move to Designated Safe Areas:

items: Interior rooms with no windows (or minimal)
Away from exterior walls if explosion risk
Central hallways or interior classrooms

steps: Account for All Persons:

items: Teachers conduct roll call
Report any missing persons to Incident Commander

steps: Monitor Situation:

items: Incident Commander monitors emergency broadcasts, authorities, weather
Updates staff via radio or phone
Prepares to evacuate if authorities order

steps: Maintain Calm:

items: Continue classes or activities if possible
Provide updates to students age-appropriately
Keep students occupied with educational activities, games

steps: Wait for "All Clear":

items: Authorities give clearance
Incident Commander announces "all clear"
Gradually resume normal operations
Battery-powered radio for emergency broadcasts
First aid kits
Water and non-perishable food
Duct tape and plastic sheeting (for sealing rooms if needed)
Flashlights and batteries
Student comfort items (books, games, blankets)

content.text: Business continuity planning ensures that learning and essential school operations can continue or be rapidly restored after a disruption. While emergency response focuses on immediate safety, business continuity focuses on operational resilience and recovery.
Identify which functions are essential to the school's mission and what they depend on.

steps: Student Supervision and Safety
content.title: Core Critical Functions (Must Continue or Restore Immediately)

items: Dependencies: Staff, safe facilities, communication systems

steps: Teaching and Learning

items: Dependencies: Teachers, curriculum materials, learning spaces (physical or digital), student access

steps: Assessment and Examinations

items: Dependencies: Exam materials, invigilators, secure storage, communication with Cambridge

steps: Student Wellbeing and Safeguarding

items: Dependencies: Counseling staff, clinic, safeguarding procedures, external support agencies

steps: Communication with Parents and Stakeholders

items: Dependencies: Phone, email, SMS systems, website

steps: Food Services
content.title: Important Functions (Must Restore Within 1-5 Days)

items: Dependencies: Kitchen facilities, food supplies, staff, health and safety standards

steps: Transportation

items: Dependencies: Vehicles, drivers, safe routes

steps: Payroll and Finance Operations

items: Dependencies: Financial systems, banking access, finance staff

steps: IT Systems and Data

items: Dependencies: Servers, internet, EduNav, backup systems

steps: Facilities Maintenance

items: Dependencies: Maintenance staff, tools and equipment, utility access

steps: Extracurricular Activities
Non-Essential Meetings and Events
New Admissions Processing (existing students prioritized)
Non-Urgent Facilities Improvements
content.title: Desirable Functions (Can Be Temporarily Suspended)

content.text: Disruptions to teaching and learning can arise from multiple causes. Your business continuity plan must address the most likely scenarios in the Indonesian context.
Causes: Fire damage, earthquake damage, flood, structural failure, hazmat contamination, extended power outage Immediate Actions (0-24 hours):

steps: Assess facility damage with Facilities Manager and authorities
Determine timeline for repairs/restoration
Decision: Can students return soon (1-2 days) or need alternate plan?
Notify GM/Foundation Representative, Yayasan, and Eduversal Central
Communicate with parents: Campus closed, further updates within [timeframe]
content.title: Scenario 1: Facility Closure (Campus Inaccessible)

items: If 1-2 Day Closure: Declare school holidays; students complete online review work
If 3-5 Day Closure: Activate online learning; teachers deliver lessons via digital platforms
If Longer Closure: Activate temporary relocation plan

content.text: Medium-Term Response (1-4 weeks): Online Learning Activation:

items: Teachers transition to online lesson delivery (video conferencing, recorded lessons, assignments via LMS)
Daily schedule adapted for online format (shorter class periods; breaks for screen time)
Not all students may have reliable internet or devices; provide alternatives:
Loan devices to students in need
Printed materials for students without internet (collection/delivery system)
Hotspot data support for students with limited connectivity
Teacher training and support: Eduversal Central DTP team provides online teaching workshops
Pastoral care: Daily check-ins; counselor availability via video or phone
Assessment adjustments: Formative focus; postpone high-stakes assessments until return
Alternative location secured (other Eduversal partner school, rented facilities, community building, mosque/church hall)
Transport arranged for students to alternative site
Essential resources relocated (textbooks, IT equipment, supplies)
Adjusted schedule (possibly shorter days; staggered entry if space limited)
Communicate clearly to parents: location, transport, schedule, what to bring
Extended relocation to alternative permanent facility
Hybrid model (some students on-site, some online; rotation if space limited)
Coordination with Yayasan and Eduversal Central for financial and logistical support
Notify Cambridge immediately if exam period affected
Arrange alternative exam venue (other Eduversal school, hotel conference room)
Secure storage and invigilation arrangements at alternative venue
Keep CIS/WASC informed of situation and continuity plan
Document disruption and response in annual report
May request delay in accreditation visit if in self-study year

content.text: Causes: Disease outbreak, transportation strike, mass resignation, labor dispute, natural disaster affecting staff commutes Immediate Actions (0-24 hours):

steps: Assess staff availability: How many teachers absent? Which subjects/grades affected?
Decision: Can school operate safely with available staff?
content.title: Scenario 2: Staff Shortage (Insufficient Teachers)

items: If Yes: Adjust schedule; combine classes; use supply teachers or substitutes
If No: School closure (follow facility closure plan)

steps: Notify parents of absence or closure

items: Recruit substitute teachers from Eduversal supply list or local agencies
Cover staff teach outside their subjects if necessary
Combine classes for some subjects
Deploy non-teaching staff for supervision roles
Online lessons delivered by absent teachers if they are well enough
Coordinate with Eduversal Central for staff rotation from other partner schools
Hire short-term contract teachers
Adjust timetable to prioritize core subjects if staff shortages continue
Increase class sizes temporarily if unavoidable
Recruitment drive for permanent staff replacements
Review and address root cause of shortage
Backup Generator: If available, covers essential loads
If No Generator or Extended Outage:
Assess: Can teaching continue safely?
If daytime and adequate natural light: Continue classes with adjusted activities
If oppressive heat or insufficient light: Early dismissal
If multiple days: School closure or half-days
content.title: Scenario 3: Utilities Failure (Power, Water, Internet)

items: If Short-Term (\<4 hours): Use stored water; limit bathroom use; early dismissal if extended
If Extended: School closure (cannot operate without sanitation)
Alternative: Water trucks for temporary supply; portable toilets
If School-Wide: Teachers adapt lessons to non-digital methods
If Extended: May not require school closure but affects EduNav access
Teachers prepare offline lesson plans as contingency

content.text: Cambridge exams are high-stakes events requiring rigorous contingency planning.

steps: Secure Alternative Exam Venue:
content.title: Pre-Exam Contingency Measures

items: Identify backup location (another Eduversal partner school, hotel conference room)
Ensure secure storage available at backup venue
Pre-arrange access and logistics

steps: Backup Exam Materials:

items: Question papers delivered well in advance; stored securely
Backup copies of timetables, seating plans, invigilator instructions

steps: Communication Plan:

items: Pre-loaded contact information for all candidates, invigilators
Alternative notification methods if EduNav down

steps: Invigilator Pool:

items: More invigilators trained than minimum required
On-call invigilators who can be deployed

content.text: Scenario: Facility Unusable on Exam Day

items: Action: Relocate to backup venue
Notification: Contact all candidates by SMS/phone
Logistics: Transport candidates; secure transport of materials
Cambridge Notification: Inform Cambridge of venue change immediately
content.title: Exam Day Contingencies

content.text: Scenario: Question Papers Lost, Damaged, or Delayed

items: Action: Contact Cambridge immediately for replacement papers
Delay: Exam may need to be postponed
Candidates Notified: As soon as new arrangements confirmed
Action:
If natural light sufficient: Continue exam
If insufficient light: Pause exam; students remain seated; track paused time
If power restored quickly: Resume with time extension
If extended outage: Postpone exam; seal papers; notify Cambridge
Action:
Immediate: "Drop, cover, hold on"; pause exam
After shaking: Assess building safety
If safe: Resume with time extension
If unsafe: Evacuate; collect papers; notify Cambridge
Illness: Student removed; allowed to finish in separate room if possible; special consideration
Cheating Suspected: Document; student completes under supervision; papers flagged
Distress/Panic: Student to quiet area; counselor support; special consideration
Student affected by illness, injury, or serious circumstances on exam day
Exam disruption due to school/venue issue
Candidate disadvantaged through no fault of their own
content.title: Cambridge Special Consideration

steps: Complete Cambridge Special Consideration form
Provide evidence (medical certificate, incident report)
Submit within 7 days
Cambridge reviews and decides

subsections.title: Data Backup and IT Disaster Recovery

content.text: EduNav and school data systems are essential to operations.

items: Student information (personal details, academic records, assessments, attendance)
Staff information (HR files, contracts, certifications)
Financial data (budget, transactions, invoices, payroll)
Communications (emails, parent notices, policies)
Curriculum documents (schemes of work, lesson plans, assessments)
Operational documents (risk registers, policies, minutes)
content.title: Data Backup Protocols

steps: EduNav Cloud Backup: Automatic daily backups by Eduversal Central
Local School Backup: Weekly backups to external hard drive in fireproof safe
Offsite Backup: Monthly backup copy stored offsite

items: Quarterly: Verify backups can be restored
Annually: Full disaster recovery test

content.text: Scenario: EduNav System Down Immediate (0-4 hours):

items: IT staff diagnose; contact Eduversal Central IT support
Notify staff: "EduNav unavailable; use offline procedures"
Teachers use paper-based registers, lesson plans
content.title: IT Disaster Recovery Plan

items: Eduversal Central resolves issue or provides workaround
If extended: Operate with offline processes
If cannot restore: Activate alternative system
Communicate with parents about alternative methods
Investigate root cause
Data migration if necessary
Disconnect affected systems immediately
Do not pay ransom
Notify Eduversal Central IT security
Assess extent of compromise
Restore from clean backups
Change all passwords
Notify affected individuals if data breached
Forensic investigation
Strengthen cybersecurity
Review data protection policies

content.text: Every critical function must have documented offline procedure:

items: Attendance: Paper registers
Lesson Plans: Backup copies on personal devices
Assessment Records: Teachers keep gradebooks in spreadsheets
Parent Communication: Phone calling tree; WhatsApp
Emergency Contacts: Printed lists in offices and classrooms
content.title: Offline Operational Procedures

content.text: Off-campus activities and educational trips enrich learning but introduce additional risks. Rigorous risk assessment and approval processes are essential.
Different activities require different levels of assessment and approval based on risk profile.

Examples: Walking field trip to nearby park/mosque/museum; local community service; guest speaker on campus; sports match at nearby school Risk Profile: Low risk; supervised by school staff; familiar environment; short duration (\<4 hours) Approval Required: Subject Leader or Year Level Coordinator; notification to Principal Risk Assessment: Simplified checklist
content.title: Category A: Low Risk \- Local, Routine Activities

content.text: Examples: Full-day trip to Jakarta attraction; inter-school sports tournament; overnight camping within province; educational tour to historical site Risk Profile: Moderate risk; requires transportation; full day or overnight; physical activity; larger group Approval Required: Principal approval in writing; notification to GM/Foundation Representative Risk Assessment: Standard risk assessment form
content.title: Category B: Medium Risk \- Extended Local or Regular Competitive Events

content.text: Examples: Mountain climbing, water sports, ropes course; international educational trip; Olympiad competition requiring air travel; outdoor education camp with challenging activities Risk Profile: Higher risk; specialized activities; expert supervision required; international travel; significant cost; extended duration Approval Required: Principal approval \+ GM/Foundation Representative approval; Yayasan notified Risk Assessment: Comprehensive risk assessment; site visit recommended; external provider vetting
content.title: Category C: High Risk \- Adventure, Specialized, or International

content.text: Every off-campus activity requires written risk assessment completed by trip leader and approved by Principal.

items: Trip title and purpose
Date(s) and duration
Destination and detailed itinerary
Category (A, B, or C)
Trip leader and accompanying staff
Student participants (number, age range, special needs)
Educational objectives and curriculum links
content.title: Risk Assessment Form Sections

content.text: 2. Hazard Identification: List all potential hazards:

items: Transportation: Road accidents, bus breakdown, driver behavior

content.text: Format: 2-3 hour facilitated discussion using scenario Example Scenarios: Scenario 1: Major Earthquake 7.0 magnitude earthquake during school hours. Structural damage to science building, multiple injuries, disrupted communications. Aftershocks expected. Parents rushing to school. Roads blocked. Discussion:

items: How assess building safety? Who decides evacuate or shelter?
How manage injured when ambulances delayed?
How communicate if internet/phone down?
Priorities in first hour? First 24 hours?
If campus unusable, where relocate? How continue learning?
How support traumatized students/staff?

content.text: Scenario 2: Serious Student Injury on International Trip Student falls at Singapore climbing facility, sustains serious injuries. Taken to hospital. Parents in Indonesia. Police investigating. Discussion:

items: Trip leader's immediate priority?
How communicate with parents?
Who travels to Singapore to support?
Legal and insurance considerations?
Manage rest of group (continue or return)?
What documentation needed?
Support classmates who witnessed?

content.text: Scenario 3: Safeguarding Allegation Against Staff Student discloses to teacher that another staff member made inappropriate physical contact. Disclosure credible and detailed. Discussion:

items: Immediate steps upon disclosure?
How ensure student safety?
Actions regarding staff member?
Who notify (authorities, Yayasan, Eduversal, parents)?
Balance confidentiality with safety?
What support does student need?
Manage investigation process?
Communication with wider community?

content.text: Scenario 4: Food Poisoning Outbreak 15+ students ill with vomiting/diarrhea after lunch. Symptoms suggest food poisoning. More showing symptoms hourly. Parents alarmed. Discussion:

items: Immediate actions for ill students?
Determine source; prevent spread?
Who investigate? What authority notify?
Communicate with parents?
Should school close? For how long?
What happens with food service?
Manage reputation and parent confidence?

steps: Introduction (15 min): Facilitator explains; scenario presented; ground rules
Initial Situation (30 min): Discuss immediate actions, priorities, roles
Injects (60 min): Facilitator introduces complications; participants discuss responses
Recovery Phase (30 min): Discuss medium/long-term; business continuity; return to normal
Debrief (30 min): What learned? Gaps identified? What do differently? Action items?

items: Document lessons learned
Update plans based on insights
Assign action items to address gaps
Share key takeaways with broader staff

content.text: Thorough documentation and analysis strengthens safety culture and drives improvement.
Purpose: Classify incidents to ensure appropriate response, reporting, and analysis. Classification Levels: Near-Miss: Event that could have caused injury/damage but did not

items: Examples: Student slips but catches self; object falls but no one nearby; fire alarm malfunction detected before false evacuation
Response: Document; investigate contributing factors; implement preventive measures

content.text: Minor Incident: Minor injury or property damage; no lasting impact

items: Examples: Small cut or scrape; bruise; minor illness (headache); small item broken
Response: First aid; parent notification if injury; logged in EduNav; no formal investigation unless pattern

content.text: Significant Incident: Moderate injury requiring medical attention; notable property damage; disruption

items: Examples: Fracture, laceration requiring stitches, serious allergic reaction; significant property damage; extended utility outage
Response: Medical treatment; parent communication; Principal notified; incident report completed; investigation if needed; risk register reviewed

content.text: Major Incident: Serious injury requiring hospitalization; major property damage; significant disruption; potential regulatory violation

items: Examples: Head injury with loss of consciousness; severe burns; hospitalization; major structural damage; hazmat spill; serious safeguarding concern

Response: Emergency services called; full incident management activated; Principal assumes command; GM/Foundation Representative notified; Yayasan informed; comprehensive investigation; formal report; regulatory notification if required; risk register updated; corrective actions implemented

content.text: Critical Incident: Fatality, permanent disability, or catastrophic damage; major reputational, legal, or regulatory implications

items: Examples: Fatality, severe permanent injury; major fire or structural collapse; serious crime; major safeguarding failure

Response: Full crisis management; emergency services; immediate notification to GM, Yayasan, Eduversal Central; external investigation (police, authorities); legal counsel engaged; media management; comprehensive review; systemic changes implemented

content.text: First Responder Actions (Any staff witnessing or first to respond):

steps: Ensure Safety: Make scene safe; remove hazards or move people from danger
Assess Severity: What happened? Anyone injured? How serious?
Provide Aid: First aid for injuries; comfort for distress
Call for Help:

items: Medical emergency: School nurse \+ ambulance if serious
Security threat: Security \+ Principal \+ police if needed

steps: Secure Scene: Preserve evidence if investigation needed
Notify Principal: Report incident to Principal or senior leader immediately
Document: Write down what you saw, heard, did as soon as possible

steps: Assess: Get facts: What happened? Who involved? Current status?
Command: Assume Incident Commander role if Level 3 or 4; delegate if Level 2
Resource: Deploy resources (ERT, external emergency services)
Communicate: Notify GM/Foundation Representative; parents of involved students; Yayasan if major; Eduversal Central if major
Coordinate: Oversee response; make decisions; adjust as situation evolves
Document: Ensure thorough documentation from beginning

content.text: Incident Report Form (completed within 24 hours for all significant and major incidents): Section 1: Basic Information

items: Incident ID (auto-generated in EduNav)
Date and time
Location (building, room, area)
Incident type (injury, illness, property damage, security, safeguarding, other)
Severity classification (near-miss, minor, significant, major, critical)

Witnesses: Names and contact information
First responder(s): Staff who responded
Report completed by: Name and role
Detailed narrative: What happened, chronologically?
Actions taken: First aid, emergency services, notifications
Outcome: Injuries sustained, property damaged, disruption caused
What directly caused the incident? (e.g., slippery floor, student running, equipment failure)
Were there conditions or behaviors that contributed? (e.g., poor lighting, lack of supervision, inadequate training, equipment not maintained)

content.text: Section 6: Root Cause Analysis (for major incidents): Use "5 Whys" technique to identify underlying root cause: Example:

items: Incident: Student fell from playground equipment and fractured arm
Why 1: Why did student fall? → Lost grip on monkey bars
Why 2: Why lose grip? → Hands were wet from rain
Why 3: Why were hands wet? → Playground equipment used during rain
Why 4: Why used during rain? → Teacher didn't know policy prohibits use when wet
Why 5: Why didn't teacher know? → New staff not trained on playground safety policy
Root Cause: Inadequate induction training for new staff on playground safety
What must be done to prevent recurrence?
Assign responsibility and deadlines
Example: "Revise staff induction checklist to include playground safety training; HR Manager; by [date]"
Who was notified and when? (parents, GM, Yayasan, authorities)
Method (phone, email, in-person)
Photos of scene/damage
Medical reports
Witness statements
Police reports (if applicable)

content.title: Risk Management to Support Insurability:

content.text: Risk management, emergency preparedness, and business continuity are essential responsibilities of school leadership. This section has provided comprehensive frameworks, protocols, and procedures to help you anticipate, prevent, and respond to threats to your school's mission of providing excellent education in a safe environment. ## Core Principles to Remember:

steps: Prevention is Best: Proactive risk identification and mitigation prevent incidents before they occur
Preparedness Saves Lives: Training, drills, and planning ensure effective response when incidents do occur
Communication is Critical: Clear, timely communication with all stakeholders during and after emergencies maintains trust and safety
Learning is Continuous: Every incident, drill, and near-miss is an opportunity to improve
Documentation Demonstrates Accountability: Thorough records show due diligence and support accreditation, compliance, and legal requirements
Safety is Everyone's Responsibility: Foster a culture where all staff and students actively contribute to safety

content.text: ## Your Role as Principal: You are the Incident Commander, the ultimate decision-maker in emergencies, and the champion of safety culture. Your visible commitment to risk management sets the tone for the entire school community. By integrating risk management into daily operations, conducting regular reviews, maintaining strong relationships with your GM/Foundation Representative and Yayasan, and empowering your Emergency Response Team, you ensure that your school is resilient, prepared, and able to maintain its educational mission even in the face of disruption. ## When in Doubt:

items: Prioritize safety over convenience
Communicate early and often
Seek expert advice
Document everything
Learn from every experience

content.text: Your students, staff, and families trust you to keep them safe. This section equips you with the knowledge and tools to honor that trust. ## Next Steps:

items: Review your current risk register and update as needed
Conduct or schedule your next emergency drill
Verify your emergency contact information is current
Confirm your Emergency Response Team members know their roles
Schedule your next risk review meeting with your GM/Foundation Representative
